title: Tests keep failing in CI but pass on my machine
body: Our CI pipeline has been randomly failing for the past few days. The test suite passes fine when I run it locally. The failures seem random — sometimes all tests pass, sometimes a handful fail. It's blocking our releases and we can't figure out what's going on.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — intermittent CI failures are frustrating, especially when they block releases.

To start narrowing this down, I need some specifics:

**What do the failing tests actually report?** Can you paste the error output (or a representative sample) from a recent failed CI run? I'm looking for the exact test names, the assertion or error messages, and any stack traces.

A link to a failed CI run would also work if the logs are accessible.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't have a link handy right now but the failing tests are all in the task ordering module — they're the new ones that got added in last week's PR. Something like TestTaskOrderAfterSort, TestInsertionOrderPreserved, that kind of thing. The errors are basically saying the output order doesn't match what's expected. Like I said, they pass fine on my machine every single time. Can we just figure out what's wrong with our CI setup?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for those details — the test names and the nature of the failures (order mismatch) are very helpful. This is starting to look like a classic non-deterministic ordering issue.

I want to test that theory before jumping to conclusions. Can you try something for me?

**Run the failing tests locally in a loop — say 50 or 100 times — and see if any fail.** Something like:

```bash
for i in $(seq 1 100); do go test ./task_ordering/... -run 'TestTaskOrderAfterSort|TestInsertionOrderPreserved' || echo "FAILED on run $i"; done
```

(Adjust for your test runner if you're not using Go.)

Also: **do these tests sort or compare ordered output, and if so, what data structure holds the tasks before the assertion?** For example, are tasks pulled from a map, a set, or an unordered query result before being sorted and compared? I'm trying to understand whether the test is asserting an order that the code doesn't actually guarantee.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Look, I'm not going to run tests in a loop 100 times, I've already spent too much time on this. They pass locally, every time, I've run them plenty. As for the data structure question — I don't know, I didn't write the original code, I just added the tests. I think the tasks go into a map somewhere before they get sorted but I'm not sure about the internals. The point is these tests worked fine when I wrote them and they work fine on my machine. Something is different about the CI environment and I need help figuring out what, not running more tests locally.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent CI failures in task ordering tests due to likely non-deterministic map iteration order","problem":"Recently added tests in the task ordering module (TestTaskOrderAfterSort, TestInsertionOrderPreserved) fail intermittently in CI with order-mismatch assertion errors, but pass consistently on the reporter's local machine. The failures are blocking releases.","root_cause_hypothesis":"Tasks are collected into a map (hash map) before being sorted and compared in test assertions. Map iteration order is non-deterministic by language spec (e.g., Go randomizes it deliberately). The tests assert a specific output order that happens to be stable on the reporter's local environment (same Go version, same hash seed) but varies in CI. If the sort is not stable (i.e., does not break ties deterministically for equal-priority tasks), the final order depends on the input order from the map, producing intermittent failures.","reproduction_steps":["Identify the tests: TestTaskOrderAfterSort, TestInsertionOrderPreserved in the task_ordering module","Run the tests in a loop (e.g., 100 iterations) — failures should appear within a few dozen runs","Inspect the test code: look for where tasks are collected from a map or unordered source before being sorted and asserted against an expected order","Check whether the sort comparator fully determines order (i.e., breaks all ties) or leaves equal elements in arbitrary order"],"environment":"CI pipeline (specific runner details not provided). Failures are environment-sensitive — consistent locally on reporter's machine, intermittent in CI. Likely related to hash seed randomization or runtime version differences between local and CI.","severity":"high","impact":"Blocking releases for the team. Every CI run is a coin flip, eroding trust in the test suite and slowing development velocity.","recommended_fix":"1. Examine the task_ordering module for map-to-slice conversions before sorting. 2. Ensure the sort comparator is total — it must break all ties deterministically (e.g., by task ID or creation timestamp as a secondary sort key). 3. Alternatively, fix the test assertions: if the code doesn't guarantee a specific order among equal-priority tasks, the tests should not assert one — use an order-independent comparison or sort both actual and expected by a stable key before comparing. 4. If using Go, consider running tests with -count=100 to verify the fix under hash seed randomization.","proposed_test_case":"Run TestTaskOrderAfterSort and TestInsertionOrderPreserved with -count=1000 (or equivalent loop). All runs must pass. Additionally, add a test that explicitly inserts tasks with identical sort keys and verifies the output is stable regardless of insertion order.","information_gaps":["Exact programming language and test framework (Go was assumed based on test naming conventions and reporter did not correct)","Exact CI runner configuration and language/runtime version vs. local","Whether the sort is stable and what the full comparator chain looks like","Whether any other tests in the module are also affected but not yet noticed"]}
